title: AI Pass — Flow Body Workstream
subtitle: Card-level AI assistance in the living-doc flow view — Cmd+K palette, structured patch, diff-before-apply.
scope: Epic #82 workstream: patch schema, skills (Claude Code + Codex), server endpoints, registry extension, and compositor UI.
sourceCoverage: Epic #82, mockup at docs/living-doc-flow-ai-pass-mockup.html, and sub-tickets #83–#88.
objective: Ship a card-level AI pass into the flow body where a user selects a card, presses Cmd+K, picks an action, reviews a structured patch, and applies accepted changes — preserving the substrate's semantic stability by making every AI-proposed change declinable.
successCondition: A user can take a thin card (like an early proof-ladder rung) from placeholder to plan end-to-end via Cmd+K → Decompose → review diff → apply, using either Claude Code or Codex as the engine, without leaving the flow view.

objectiveFacets.name: Registry as guardrail
objectiveFacets.description: Every AI-proposed change is validated against its convergence-type contract before it reaches the user.

objectiveFacets.name: User guards intent
objectiveFacets.description: The diff is the review gate; each proposed change is individually declinable.

objectiveFacets.name: Semantic stability preserved
objectiveFacets.description: AI may not silently mutate governance structure — facets, coverage, invariants, and type boundaries stay trustworthy.

objectiveFacets.name: Cross-engine parity
objectiveFacets.description: Works with Claude Code and Codex alike; user picks the engine when both are installed.

objectiveFacets.name: Local only, no browser keys
objectiveFacets.description: No API keys in the HTML. Engine CLIs hold credentials; the local doc server is the bridge.

invariants.name: Every patch is validated before it reaches the UI
invariants.statement: The validator from #83 runs on every patch before any diff is rendered. A patch with contract violations is rejected or annotated, never silently applied.

invariants.name: Every proposed change is individually declinable
invariants.statement: The diff UI must expose a per-change checkbox. No bundle-apply without the ability to deselect individual changes.

invariants.name: No engine credentials live in the browser
invariants.statement: All engine invocations are shelled out by the local server to the CLI tool. The HTML never carries an API key or a provider token.

callouts.title: How to read this doc
items: Status Snapshot is the landing view — where the work is right now.
Architecture Decisions are settled calls. Challenge a card, don't create a new one.
Actions Inventory grows as type-specific actions get specified and shipped.
Components are revision-pinned anchors that flip from 'not-built' to 'current' as code lands.
Attempts tracks skill-prompt iterations once we start tuning per engine.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.rationale: One scannable view of where the workstream is. Counts and one callout — not a copy of the epic's checklist.

callout.title: Current edge
items: End-to-end loop proven on real hardware — Cmd+K → propose → diff → apply.
2 general actions exercised live: summarise (read-only) and enrich-notes (self-mutation).
Next work: type-specific action handlers. The skills list 14 across 9 convergence types but implementations are stubs.
10 attempts logged — 6 ship, 3 dogfooding refinements, 1 capstone (self-mutation).

sections.title: Architecture Decisions
sections.rationale: Five calls settled during the proposal conversation. Captured so the next session does not re-litigate; if an implementation challenges a decision, the card gets updated rather than duplicated.

data.name: Transport: extend the local doc server
notes: Endpoints /api/ai-pass/propose, /apply, /engines live on the existing server that already serves docs at localhost:4321. No separate daemon.
Rationale: engine credentials stay in the CLI. The browser never sees an API key, which matches the local-only principle.

data.name: Batch, not streaming, for v1
notes: Spinner during propose; render the completed patch when it arrives. Streaming inline edits would break the user-guards-intent contract — a partial patch is an invitation to premature apply.
Revisit for v2 if latency feels too opaque in practice.

data.name: Two separate skills, one per engine
notes: Claude Code and Codex each get their own skill file. Not a shared lowest-common-denominator prompt.
Tradeoff accepted: some duplication between skills, in exchange for provider-specific prompt tuning.

data.name: create-ticket changes apply without extra confirmation
notes: The overall Apply button gates the whole patch. A ticket-create change is unchecked like any other if the user wants to decline it in the diff.
Philosophy: people can always dive into the created ticket on GitHub and correct or close it. Avoid over-gating.

data.name: Engine preference persists server-side
notes: Shared across browser tabs and devices, matching the library manifest's persistence.
Written via PUT /api/ai-pass/engines/preferred; read via GET /api/ai-pass/engines.

sections.title: Actions Inventory
sections.rationale: The action set shown in the palette is a merged view of general actions (hardcoded) and type-specific actions (declared per convergence type in the registry via #84). This section is the canonical list; new convergence types declare their own aiActions there and appear here once documented.

data.name: Decompose into sub-cards (general)
notes: Umbrella card → N scoped sub-cards, new tickets via gh, coverage edges added. Mirrors the L4 proof-ladder decomposition done by hand in this session.

data.name: Enrich notes (general)
notes: Thin card → richer prose with callouts and references, matching the style of neighbour cards in the same section.

data.name: Verify against invariants (general)
notes: Check the card against applicable invariants; flag violations with the specific invariant id and quote the rule.

data.name: Propose coverage edges (general)
notes: Map the card to facets it carries. Surface orphan facets honestly — do not fabricate coverage.

data.name: Summarise (general)
notes: Concise one-paragraph summary of the card's current state. Read-only — never writes.

data.name: Type-specific action handlers (14 across 9 types) — priority subset for v2
notes: Highest leverage (recommend v2 priority order):
1. code-anchor → check-revision-drift. Concrete git check, detectable drift. Most useful diagnostic.
2. attempt-log → find-shipping-commit. Auto-links shipped workarounds to commits.
3. issue-orbit → refresh-github-state. Keeps orbit cards honest without manual updates.
4. capability-surface → propose-status-from-commits. Flips status automatically when codePaths get touched.
5. maintainer-stance → check-evolution. Pulls thread updates into the stance card.
Remaining stubs (still declared in registry, not yet prompted in skills):
code-anchor → propose-replacement-anchor
attempt-log → propose-supersession
symptom-observation → suggest-environment-variants, check-contradictions
issue-orbit → reclassify-relationship
proof-ladder → check-monotonic-invariant
decision-record → check-if-still-current
investigation-findings → check-still-holding

sections.title: Components
sections.rationale: As the schema, validator, server endpoints, skills, and UI land, each gets a revision-pinned anchor here. Currently every card is marked changed-since-issue because the code has not yet been written; they flip to current when the respective sub-ticket closes.

data.name: Patch schema — living-doc-ai-patch/v1
data.what_it_does: JSON schema describing every change kind in the patch format emitted by skills and consumed by the server

why_it_matters.text: Every other component reads or writes this shape. If the schema churns, every downstream piece churns with it. Land first.

data.name: Patch validator
data.what_it_does: Validates a patch against the schema and against the target convergence-type contracts (required fields, allowed status values, orphan-facet detection)

why_it_matters.text: This is where semantic stability is enforced. If the validator is weak, AI can drift type boundaries silently and the substrate loses its guarantees.

data.name: Registry aiActions extension
data.what_it_does: Declares per-type AI actions so the palette can merge them with the general set

why_it_matters.text: New convergence types opt into AI help by declaring their own actions. No renderer, palette, or server changes required to add a new action to an existing type.

data.name: Server endpoints for AI pass
data.what_it_does: Shells out to engine CLI, validates the returned patch, applies accepted changes (including gh issue create for ticket-create changes), re-renders the doc

why_it_matters.text: The only component with authority to invoke gh and mutate JSON on disk. Must be airtight — it is the blast-radius boundary for AI-originated changes.

Mutation is split out into a pure apply module (apply-ai-patch.mjs) so the substrate-mutating logic is unit-testable without standing up the HTTP transport. Side effects like ticket-create surface from apply as commands the server runs.

The engine CLI is spawned with cwd set to the target doc's repo root (walked up from docPath to find .git), not living-doc-compositor. Without this, gh issue create would default to the wrong remote and relative code anchors would resolve against the wrong tree.

The registry is passed inline in the engine request payload instead of having skills reach for scripts/living-doc-registry.json in cwd — necessary once cwd became the target repo rather than this one.

A three-tier tolerant JSON parser (direct parse → fenced code block → first balanced {...}) absorbs engines that wrap output in ``` fences even when told not to. A strict contract looked correct in isolation but broke in practice.

data.name: Skill: living-doc-ai-pass-claude
data.what_it_does: Claude-tuned skill that reads doc + registry + neighbourhood and emits a v1 patch on stdout

why_it_matters.text: Tuned for Claude's strengths: long-form prose, decomposition, structure preservation. Free to diverge from the Codex skill prompt.

data.name: Skill: living-doc-ai-pass-codex
data.what_it_does: Codex-tuned skill emitting the same v1 patch contract

why_it_matters.text: Tuned for Codex's strengths: terse, code-anchor-oriented checks, revision comparison. Free to diverge from the Claude skill prompt.

data.name: Cmd+K palette + diff sidebar UI
data.what_it_does: Interactive surface: Cmd+K opens the action palette, spinner during propose, diff slides in, per-change accept/decline, apply submits accepted changes

why_it_matters.text: The UX the user actually sees. Static mockup at docs/living-doc-flow-ai-pass-mockup.html is the spec.

sections.title: Attempts
sections.rationale: Records iterations on skill prompts, engine-specific behaviour differences, and probes that narrowed design decisions. Empty at workstream start; grows as we build and observe.

data.name: Patch schema v1 shipped with three-layer validator
data.cost_to_apply: ~690 LOC across schema, validator, and contract spec. No new deps (no Ajv).

what_tried.text: Hand-rolled shape validation against a draft-07 schema (no Ajv) + registry contract checks + doc consistency checks + orphan detection via patch simulation.

what_proved.text: A three-layer validator fits the substrate model: shape for structure, registry for type boundaries, doc for references. Orphan detection via coverage simulation works cleanly.

data.name: generalAiActions + per-type aiActions declared in registry
data.cost_to_apply: 5 general actions + 14 type-specific across 9 types. Contract spec validates shape.

what_tried.text: Kept the schema minimal: aiAction is { id, name, description }. Scope is implicit (general at root, type-specific per convergenceType).

what_proved.text: Registry stays data-driven: adding a new action to an existing type is a JSON edit + sync, no code change. New convergence types can declare their own aiActions without touching the palette.

data.name: Claude Code skill emits v1 patches per action
data.cost_to_apply: ~300 LOC SKILL.md. No code — instructions only. Claude Code reads the file and runs.

what_tried.text: Structured the skill around (1) gather context, (2) pick handler, (3) emit JSON-only. Included examples for each general action and a self-check step that mirrors the validator layers.

what_proved.text: Concrete examples + explicit principles + the validator downstream is enough. The skill does not need to be code — the contract (schema) is the enforcement, the prompt is the steering.

data.name: Codex skill emits v1 patches with provider-tuned prompts
data.cost_to_apply: ~230 LOC SKILL.md. Content-identical contract with Claude skill; prose tuned for Codex.

what_tried.text: Kept the output contract pixel-identical to the Claude skill; diverged only in prose guidance (terse over lyrical, code-anchor examples lead, rationales one sentence).

what_proved.text: Two-skills-one-contract holds: the server is engine-agnostic, the validator is the common gate, providers can drift in style without breaking the substrate.

data.name: AI-pass server + pure apply module shipped, smoke-tested
data.cost_to_apply: ~600 LOC across server + apply + spec. Zero new deps.

what_tried.text: Split mutation (pure apply-ai-patch.mjs) from transport (HTTP server). Side effects like ticket-create surface from apply as commands the server runs — keeps apply unit-testable.
Engine command is config-driven (~/.living-doc-compositor/ai-pass-config.json) so the CLI shape can change per provider without touching the server.

what_proved.text: End-to-end loop works with a mock engine: GET /engines returns available engines, POST /propose validates + stores a patch, POST /apply mutates the JSON and re-renders. Actual engine integration is a config tweak, not a code change.

data.name: Cmd+K palette + diff sidebar UI shipped; epic complete
data.cost_to_apply: ~400 LOC (CSS + JS) appended to the compositor, propagated to every rendered doc on re-render.

what_tried.text: Self-contained module at the bottom of the compositor HTML. CSS prefixed with ap- to avoid collisions. Reads registry via window.LD_REGISTRY (exposed from the main IIFE). Hooks into flow-view selection via .l2-card-selected.

what_proved.text: The UX mockup (docs/living-doc-flow-ai-pass-mockup.html) translated cleanly to a working widget: Cmd+K → palette → propose → diff → apply. All six sub-tickets of epic #82 are shipped.

data.name: Path resolution cascade across four contexts
data.cost_to_apply: ~60 LOC across palette + dashboard backend route.

what_tried.text: First assumed #doc-meta would be present. It is not, when the doc is loaded inside the compositor overlay iframe via srcdoc — the overlay is a fresh compositor shell.

Cascade built: (1) window.LD_DOC_ABS_PATH injected by prjctmem dashboard backend; (2) window.parent.LD_DOC_ABS_PATH when running inside the srcdoc overlay; (3) localStorage override for manual testing; (4) live compositor state via window.LD_GET_STATE; (5) static #doc-meta fallback.

what_proved.text: Path resolution must treat the palette as a module that can run in any of four contexts: outer dashboard doc, compositor overlay iframe (srcdoc), standalone rendered doc at file://, and standalone compositor edit page. A single mechanism was never going to fit.
Backend injection is the right primary — it makes the path authoritative rather than inferred. Same-origin parent-window read handles the srcdoc overlay without extra plumbing.

data.name: Server robustness — repo-root cwd, inline registry, tolerant JSON
data.cost_to_apply: ~90 LOC in ai-pass-server.mjs. Skill SKILL.md files updated to read registry from input.

what_tried.text: Derive the target doc repo root by walking up for .git from the absolute docPath. Spawn the engine CLI with that cwd so gh/git commands and relative code paths resolve against the right repo.

Pass the registry inline in the engine request payload instead of the skill reaching for scripts/living-doc-registry.json in cwd — necessary because cwd is now the target repo, not living-doc-compositor.
Three-tier tolerant JSON parser on the engine response: direct parse → fenced code block → first balanced {...}. Engines wrap output in  fences even when told not to.

what_proved.text: The skill contract and the transport have to tolerate each other. A strict contract that fails on prose wrappers looks correct in isolation but breaks in practice — engines are messy.

Cwd matters. Running the engine in living-doc-compositor when the doc lives in a different repo would have meant gh issue create defaulted to the wrong remote, git diffs looked wrong, and code-anchor paths did not resolve.

data.name: UX — single sidebar surface for every state

what_tried.text: v1 had a floating top-right spinner and bottom toast for errors. Three UI surfaces with different grammars confused the user — spinner appeared away from where they were looking, errors disappeared too fast to read.

Consolidated to one surface: the diff sidebar opens the moment an action fires and transitions through loading → diff → applying → applied, or → error. Errors render inside the sidebar with a scrollable monospace box so the full server response is visible.
Polish pass: summary in its own muted-bg callout instead of the h3, Apply button only renders when there are changes to apply, font family unified across palette + sidebar + all states.

what_proved.text: User-guards-intent demands that every state be somewhere the user is already looking. The diff sidebar is the canonical surface for AI pass — the palette is just the trigger.
No-changes outcomes (like summarise) should not show an Apply button at all. Grey-disabled buttons read as broken rather than intentional.

data.name: First real apply: the doc updates itself via its own skill system
data.shipped_in: manual run from the palette (no commit yet)
data.cost_to_apply: 0 LOC — it was already shipped. First live exercise.

what_tried.text: User selected the server-endpoints card in the Flow view, pressed Cmd+K, picked enrich-notes, waited for the engine to propose, accepted the diff, hit Apply.

what_proved.text: Every piece of epic #82 functions on real hardware: schema validator + registry actions + Claude Code skill + server /propose + /apply + palette + diff sidebar. The substrate is now self-hosting — a living doc just refined itself through the skill system it describes.
The dogfood signal from the pitch (the AI pass, once built, should operate on its own doc) is now concrete. This attempt card itself was authored after the self-mutation landed.

data.name: Bring the doc to reality — next work surfaces on its own

what_tried.text: Read the living doc, spotted stale Status Snapshot (still claimed 6 open sub-tickets) and all six Actions Inventory cards marked not-built despite two having been exercised live. Flipped summarise + enrich to built, decompose/verify/coverage to partial, and promoted the type-specific-stubs card from a dump list to a priority-ordered subset with specific v2 targets.

what_proved.text: The doc really is the work surface: once it matched reality, the unimplemented type-specific actions lit up as the single obvious next chunk. No external backlog needed.

A living doc left alone goes stale in hours — attempts log drifts out of sync with card statuses, snapshots point at yesterday. The /living-doc freshness discipline and eventually an AI-pass verify action are the counterweights.
